author: The Fourth Ecumenical Council. The Council of Chalcedon
book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.

classification.cautionReason: Conciliar or creedal material received in mainstream Nicene Christian tradition, with denominational differences on some canons.

lineage: The Fourth Ecumenical Council. The Council of Chalcedon.
The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.

metadata.author: The Fourth Ecumenical Council. The Council of Chalcedon

title: Canon XXIV.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Monasteries, which have once been consecrated with the consent of the bishop, shall remain monasteries for ever, and the property belonging to them shall be preserved, and they shall never again become secular dwellings.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: And they who shall permit this to be done shall be liable to ecclesiastical penalties.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Ancient Epitome of Canon XXIV.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: A monastery erected with the consent of the bishop shall be immovable.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: And whatever pertains to it shall not be alienated.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Whoever shall take upon him to do otherwise, shall not be held guiltless.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Joseph Ægyptius, in turning this into Arabic, reads: “And whoever shall turn any monastery into a dwelling house for himself…let him be cursed and anathema.”

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: The curious reader is referred on this whole subject to Sir Henry Spelman’s History and Fate of Sacrilege, or to the more handy book on the subject by James Wayland Joyce, The Doom of Sacrilege.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: The secularization of monasteries was an evil which grew with their wealth and influence.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: At a Council held by the patriarch Photius in the Apostles’ church at Constantinople, it is complained that some persons attach the name of “monastery” to property of their own, and while professing to dedicate it to God, write themselves down as lords of what has been thus consecrated, and are not ashamed to claim after such consecration the same power over it which they had before.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: In the West, we find this abuse attracting the attention of Gregory the Great, who writes to a bishop that “rationalis ordo” would not allow a layman to pervert a monastic foundation at will to his own uses (Epist. viii., 31).

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: In ancient Scotland, the occasional dispersion of religious communities, and, still more, the clan-principle which assigned chieftain-rights over monasteries to the descendants of the founder, left at Dunkeld, Brechin, Abernethy, and elsewhere, “nothing but the mere name of abbacy applied to the lands, and of abbot borne by the secular lord for the time” (Skene’s Celtic Scotland, ii., 365; cf.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Anderson’s Scotland in Early Christian Times, p.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: So, after the great Irish monastery of Bangor in Down was destroyed by the Northmen, “non defuit,” says St. Bernard, “qui illud teneret cure possessionibus suis; nam et constituebantur per electionem etiam, et abbates appellabantur, servantes nomine, etsi non re, quod olim exstiterat” (De Vita S.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Malachiæ, vj.).

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: So in 1188 Giraldus Cambrensis found a lay abbot in possession of the venerable church of Llanbadarn Vawr; a “bad custom,” he says, “had grown up, whereby powerful laymen, at first chosen by the clergy to be “œconomi” or “patroni et defensores,” had usurped “totum jus,” appropriated the lands, and left to the clergy nothing but the altars, with tithes and offerings (Itin.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Camb. ii., 4).

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: This abuse must be distinguished from the corrupt device whereby, in Bede’s later years, Northumbrian nobles contrived to gain for their estates the immunities of abbey-lands by professing to found monasteries, which they filled with disorderly monks, who lived there in contempt of all rule (Bede, Ep. to Egbert, vij.).

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: In the year of his birth, the first English synod had forbidden bishops to despoil consecrated monasteries (Bede, iv., 5).

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: This canon is found in the Corpus Juris Canonici, Gratian’s Decretum, Pars II., Causa XIX., Quæst.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: III., canon iv.
